meta.title: F21 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series F21 engine — N13, N20, N55, N47, B38, B48, B58, B37, B47. Real UK costs, timing‑chain risks, and M135i/M140i data from 20+ vetted BMW specialists.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series F21 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F21 engine typically costs £1,500–£7,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the volume N47D20C diesel sits at £3,200–£5,500, while the B58 in the M140i is £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure based on your variant and engine code.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series F21 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30A in the M140i (2016–2019) is the most reliable engine in the F21 range — BMW's modular straight‑six is chain‑driven, robust, and has few reported major failures. On the diesel side, the B47D20A (post‑2015 118d/120d) is the most dependable, with a front‑mounted timing chain that's far more accessible than the N47's rear‑mounted design.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series F21?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N47 timing chain stretch (pre‑2015 diesel fours) is the biggest issue — cold‑start rattle is the warning. The N13 timing chain tensioner wear affects pre‑2015 petrol fours, and the N55 HPFP and injector failure is the M135i's defining risk. On LCI three‑cylinder models (B38/B37), engine mount wear is a common but minor issue.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series F21 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which side of the 2015 facelift your car sits on. Pre‑LCI cars with N47 or N13 engines carry a genuine timing‑chain risk — budget for inspection or replacement. Post‑LCI cars with B‑series engines (B38, B48, B58, B37, B47) are significantly more reliable, with chain issues largely resolved. The B58 in the M140i is considered exceptionally robust.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series F21 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't say 'avoid' outright — but a pre‑2015 N47D20C without a documented timing‑chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A pre‑2015 N13 without chain history is the second. A neglected N55 M135i without HPFP history is also a gamble.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F21 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold‑start rattle (N47/N13), long cranking or limp mode (N55 HPFP), or blue smoke and rough idle (any high‑mileage engine) are the key warning signs. On three‑cylinder LCI cars, excessive vibration may indicate mount wear, not engine failure. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series F21 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £10,000 F21, a £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon is 32–55% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 80–137% — that crosses our 70% BMW threshold, so we'd advise walking away. For an M135i or M140i, the higher retained values make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 1 Series F21 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 six‑cylinder can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — it's one of BMW's most durable modern engines. The B47 and B48 fours are similarly robust. The N47 and N13's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, either can reach 150,000+ miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F20 and F21?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F20 and F21 are the same generation of BMW 1 Series — the F20 is the 5‑door hatchback, while the F21 is the 3‑door hatchback. Both share the same platform, engine range, and interior. This page covers the F21 specifically, but all engine data applies equally to the F20. The F40 (2019–present) is the successor, built on a front‑wheel‑drive platform.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series F21 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. Common F21 codes include N13B16A (114i/116i/118i pre‑LCI), N20B20 (125i), N55B30A (M135i), B38B15A (116i/118i LCI), B48B20A (120i LCI), B58B30A (M140i), N47D16A/D20C/D20D (114d/116d/118d/120d/125d pre‑LCI), B37D15A (114d/116d LCI), B47D20A/B (118d/120d/125d LCI). Tell us your variant and registration date (pre‑ or post‑July 2015) and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: BMW 1 Series F21 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the 1 Series F21 three‑door — from the entry‑level 114i to the fire‑breathing M135i and M140i six‑cylinders, and the full range of N47 and B47 diesel fours that define UK ownership. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the 2015 facelift engine refresh that brought BMW’s modular B‑series engines to the range. Backed by 344+ real UK F20/F21 enquiries in 2025 [BMW‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series F21 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F21 three‑door — from the pre‑LCI N‑series engines to the post‑2015 B‑series modular family that brought three‑cylinder power to the range.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20C: 95% confidence — 1,450 JLR‑wide [EM‑VERIFIED] figures; reliability rating inherited from the Model Page. B47D20A: 90% confidence — 930 JLR‑wide [EM‑VERIFIED] figures; reliability rating inherited from Model Page. N13, N20, N55, B38, B48, B58, B37: Expert‑knowledge tier — ratings inherited from the Model Page where available. B58 in the M140i is considered the most reliable engine in the F21 range, with a 5‑star rating inherited from the Model Page.

overview.intro: The BMW 1 Series F21 is the three‑door hatchback variant of the second‑generation 1 Series, produced from September 2011 to May 2019. Built on BMW’s L7 platform and featuring rear‑wheel drive for most models (with xDrive all‑wheel drive available as an option), the F21 offered a wide range of turbocharged petrol and diesel engines. A significant 2015 facelift (LCI) brought BMW’s modular B‑series engine family to the range, replacing the N‑series engines with more efficient three‑ and four‑cylinder units. The range was topped by the M135i and later M140i, featuring 3.0‑litre six‑cylinder turbocharged engines that made the F21 one of the most engaging hot hatchbacks of its era.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L7 • Predecessor: BMW 1 Series E87/E81 • Successor: BMW 1 Series F40 • Sibling body styles in this generation: F20 (5‑door hatchback).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The BMW 1 Series F20/F21 generated 344 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the N47 diesel fours as the parc exits affordable‑warranty age and timing‑chain concerns come to the fore.

items.quote: “The B47 is BMW’s modular diesel four — front‑mounted timing chain, far more accessible than the N47’s rear‑mounted design, and generally regarded as a strong and dependable engine capable of high mileage if properly maintained.” Who it’s for: buyers who want diesel economy without the N47’s timing‑chain anxiety.

“The B58 is BMW’s modular straight‑six masterpiece — 340 bhp, silky smooth, and chain‑driven with a robust bottom end. It’s the engine that made the M140i the hot hatch benchmark. The most reliable engine in the F21 range.” Who it’s for: buyers who want six‑cylinder performance without the N55’s fuel‑pump anxieties.

“The rear‑mounted timing chain is the defining risk of the pre‑LCI diesel four — cold‑start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. The N47 is the single biggest ownership risk on the F21.” Who it’s for: only buyers with documented chain‑replacement history.

“The high‑output N47 in the 125d carries the same rear‑chain risk as the lower‑output variants, but with more power and higher stresses. A snapped chain in a 125d means the same £3,500–£6,000 replacement.” Who it’s for: 125d buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

“The B47 is the N47’s successor — front‑mounted chain, better oil supply, and significantly lower risk of catastrophic failure. It’s the diesel four you can buy without anxiety.” Who it’s for: buyers who want modern diesel efficiency without the N47’s caveats.

“The B48 is the four‑cylinder petrol that replaced the N13 — 184 bhp, chain‑driven, and significantly more reliable than the N13 it replaced. It’s the sweet spot of the petrol range.” Who it’s for: buyers who want petrol refinement without the M140i’s running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history
Performance Choice — fast, costlier to maintain
Best Petrol — the M140i’s defining asset
Highest Risk — chain is the limiting factor
Highest Risk — cold‑start rattle is the warning
Most Expensive — more power, same risk

economicsRule.title: The F21 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £4,000–£12,000 F21 sits at 27–137% of the car’s value. On a sub‑£5,000 F21, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we’ll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £10,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 32–55% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Stretch (pre‑2015 diesel fours)
problems.rootCause: The rear‑mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold‑start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. This affects the entire N47 family, including the high‑output N47D20D in the 125d.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle on any N47‑powered F21, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an F21 worth under £5,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your F21 →

problems.title: N13 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (pre‑2015 petrol fours)
problems.rootCause: The N13 (BMW’s version of the Prince engine) can suffer from timing chain tensioner wear, leading to chain slack and rattle on start‑up. If ignored, the chain can jump or snap, causing catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: On any pre‑2015 petrol F21, have the timing chain tensioner inspected at 60,000 miles — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,500 replacement that follows a snapped chain. The N13 is otherwise a robust engine, but this single weak point defines its ownership experience.

cta.label: Compare N13 engine prices for your F21 →

problems.title: N55 HPFP & Injector Failure (M135i)
problems.rootCause: The high‑pressure fuel pump’s internal wear leads to fuel pressure loss, hard starting, and eventual engine damage if ignored; injector issues frequently follow. This is the M135i’s defining ownership cost risk.
problems.recommendation: If your M135i has no documented HPFP history, treat it as overdue preventative maintenance — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a lean‑running failure. This is the single most important maintenance item on the N55.

cta.label: Compare N55 engine prices for your M135i →

problems.title: B38/B37 Engine Mount Wear (LCI three‑cylinders)
problems.rootCause: The three‑cylinder engines (B38 petrol, B37 diesel) are inherently less balanced than four‑ or six‑cylinder units. This can lead to premature wear of the engine mounts, resulting in increased vibration and noise in the cabin.
problems.recommendation: This is a minor but common issue on three‑cylinder F21s — a £200–£400 mount replacement restores the car’s refinement and is one of the cheapest fixes on the range. The B38 and B37 engines themselves are robust and chain‑driven; this is the only recurring issue.

cta.label: Compare B38/B37 engine mount replacement quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: F21 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page’s baseline figures — F21 shares its engine‑bay layout with the F20 5‑door, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. N47 and B47 costs are carried forward from the Model Page’s existing cost tables.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are F21 3‑door hatchback only. The 125i was offered with the N20B20 engine producing 218 bhp (pre‑LCI) and later 238 bhp. The M135i (pre‑LCI) used the N55B30A six‑cylinder producing 326 bhp. The M140i (LCI) used the B58B30A producing 340 bhp. The 120i was offered with the N13 (pre‑LCI, 177 bhp) and later the B48 (LCI, 184 bhp). The 125d was offered with the N47D20D (pre‑LCI, 218 bhp) and later the B47D20B (LCI, 224 bhp). The 114d and 116d were offered with the N47D16A/N47D20C (pre‑LCI) and later the B37D15A (LCI).
